food_name: Pecel (Javanese steamed vegetable salad with peanut sauce)
aliases: blanched vegetable salad with peanut sauce

english: blanched vegetable salad with peanut sauce

food_subgroup: Steamed/blanched mixed vegetables with peanut-spice sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no concept for Pecel. Closest parent is blanched vegetable salad with peanut-based sauce dressing. No exact FoodOn URI available; null is appropriate.
ontology.foodex2_description: Blanched mixed vegetable salad with peanut-spice sauce (Indonesian pecel style)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00WY used as closest parent for mixed vegetable salad with sauce; no exact pecel-specific code exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for pecel or Javanese vegetable salad with peanut sauce.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of pecel is approximately 200g, comprising a generous portion of blanched mixed vegetables dressed with peanut sauce, typically served alongside steamed rice or lontong.

nutrient_source.title: Pecel (Javanese peanut vegetable salad) — FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Regional Food Composition Table; cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD component data
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Regional Food Composition Table; Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived from FAO/INFOODS SEA regional table for blanched mixed vegetable salad with peanut-based sauce, cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD data for individual components (spinach, bean sprouts, long beans, peanut sauce). Values represent a composite estimate for the complete dish as typically prepared in East Java. No single exact pecel entry exists in any approved source; values are adapted from component-level data.

gi_source.title: University of Sydney GI Database — analogous estimate for blanched mixed vegetable salad with peanut sauce; majority of carbohydrates from low-GI vegetables (spinach, bean sprouts, long beans); peanut fat and dietary fibre further attenuate glycaemic response
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for pecel exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 38 is an analogous estimate based on the GI of blanched mixed vegetables (GI ~15–30) and peanut-based sauce (GI ~14 for peanuts). Palm sugar contributes a moderate GI (~50) but in small quantity. The composite GI of 38 is defensible as low-GI. GL per 200g serving = (38 × 8.5 × 2) / 100 = 6.46, rounded to 6.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 210 mg per 100g from salt in peanut sauce
hypertension.guidance: A standard 200g serving delivers approximately 420 mg sodium, which is moderate; individuals with hypertension should monitor portion size and request reduced-salt peanut sauce, while the potassium content (290 mg/100g) from the vegetable mix provides a beneficial counterbalancing effect on blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycaemic index (38) and low glycaemic load per serving (6.5) from predominantly vegetable carbohydrates
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pecel is well-suited for individuals with type 2 diabetes due to its low GI (38), low GL per serving (6.5), high dietary fibre (3.5 g/100g), and the fat from peanuts which further attenuates postprandial glucose response; palm sugar in the sauce has a moderate GI (~50) but is present in small amounts.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (1.5 g/100g), zero trans fat, and predominantly unsaturated fat from peanuts
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pecel presents a favourable cardiovascular profile with low saturated fat (1.5 g/100g), zero trans fat, zero cholesterol, and peanut-derived fat that is predominantly monounsaturated and polyunsaturated; the high folate (65 mcg DFE/100g), vitamin C (18 mg/100g), and dietary fibre (3.5 g/100g) further support cardiovascular health, though sodium intake should be monitored.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory micronutrient profile from diverse vegetable mix
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Pecel's diverse vegetable base provides folate (65 mcg DFE/100g), vitamin C (18 mg/100g), vitamin A (145 mcg RAE/100g), and magnesium (40 mg/100g), all of which support anti-inflammatory pathways; the predominantly unsaturated fat from peanuts is consistent with an anti-inflammatory dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pecel is a beloved Javanese vegetable salad dish and one of the classic everyday foods of East and Central Java, Indonesia. It consists of an assortment of blanched or steamed vegetables — commonly spinach, bean sprouts, long beans, and cassava leaves — dressed generously with a thick, aromatic peanut sauce made from ground roasted peanuts, kencur (aromatic ginger), palm sugar, tamarind, lime leaves, and dried chilli. The sauce delivers a distinctive earthy-sweet-spicy-sour balance that is characteristic of Javanese cuisine. Pecel is commonly served on steamed rice (nasi pecel) or with lontong (compressed rice cake). Pecel Madiun from East Java is regarded as the most iconic regional variant, characterised by its fragrant kencur-forward peanut sauce. Nutritionally, pecel is excellent: it is low glycaemic (GI 38, GL 6.5 per 200g serving), high in dietary fibre (3.5 g/100g), rich in vitamins A, C, E, folate, and minerals including potassium, calcium, magnesium, and iron. Despite the peanut sauce, saturated fat is low (1.5 g/100g) and the dish is fully plant-based, making it suitable for vegetarian, vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free diets. It is well-suited for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and hypertension when portion size and sauce salt content are managed.

confidence_reason: No single exact pecel entry exists in any approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from FAO/INFOODS SEA regional table and IMR Malaysia MyFCD component-level data for the individual vegetables and peanut sauce. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on component foods. Confidence is medium rather than high because the values represent a composite adaptation rather than a direct approved-source match.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for pecel as a composite dish is not available in FAO/INFOODS SEA or IMR Malaysia MyFCD as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish is not reported in the approved sources consulted. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — plant-based dish with no hydrogenated fats). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — fully plant-based dish). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no vitamin D-containing ingredients). All core macros (protein_g 5.5, fat_g 9.0, carbohydrate_g 8.5) are non-zero and sourced from FAO/INFOODS SEA and IMR Malaysia MyFCD component data. FoodEx2 code A00WY used as closest parent; no exact pecel-specific FoodEx2 code exists. GI of 38 is an analogous estimate; no direct pecel GI measurement exists in the University of Sydney GI Database.
